sentences.text: I was four.
How old?
Four years old.
Around four.
I remember the house that we lived in.
It had red painted floor.
It was a fair-sized room.
It had a table, two beds.
It had a Dutch oven, with a little place to go in-- oh, excuse me.
[DRINKS] And it had an entry room, earth floor.
The door was with straw and burlap, to keep the warmth.
In the wintertime the snow was so bad that it covered up the windows.
I remember my sled, which was [LAUGHS] a round bowl filled with ice, froze overnight.
And in that entry room, we had a goat.
We had a goat.
And her name was Katya, like all the goats' names was Katya.
And at first, my mother, to identify this white goat, she made three marks with a pencil on her-- those ink pencils.
In those days we had ink pencils.
You had to wet it, in order to write ink.
She marked it, three stripes.
It just so happened that it rained.
We couldn't find our Katya.
And the shepherd would come through the main road in the morning, and everybody let out their goats, cows, whatever, take this to the fields.
And he would bring it home at night, through the same route.
And the animals knew which doors to go into.
And Katya came home.
And we kept Katya in that-- in that entry room.
And she wouldn't let herself be milked.
So my mother would tie her horns very close to the wall, and she would push her right to the wall, so she wouldn't turn over the bucket of milk.
[LAUGHS] And from that milk, my mother always made all sorts of things that she learned how to do.
She was a city girl.
She didn't know anything about these things.
But she learned fairly quickly.
And she made cheeses.
And she made kefir, and she made sour cream.
And she learned how to bake, and oh.
Well, after my father was gone, she learned to bake from potatoes, at least 40 different things, just potatoes.
Oh, yes.
I loved it.
I loved it.
I used to milk those little-- [LAUGHS] eventually, Katya became my goat.
But-- but till then, she was-- she was our main, main source of protein.
So it was kind of nice to have Katya.
It was fun, you know, as a child, you had a goat, we had a couple of chickens.
You know, it was-- it was lively.
You know, there was livestock around.
And in those days my father was working in a-- in that job where he got the-- [NON-ENGLISH], the factory, the job, the, the-- I guess it was a factory.
And because he was well liked and he was doing very well, we were fairly well provided.
In the kolkhoz, everybody has some land that you work for yourself.
And then through trade, one way or the other, we had a little bit of flour, and we had a little bit of this, a little bit of that.
For the winter, you put away your potatoes in the cellar, cabbage, that sort of thing.
We also had dandelions.
We also had spinach growing wildly.
So it was not too bad, not-- I had no comparison to make.
[BACKROUND NOISE]
In-- on the kolkhoz, oh yes.
On the kolkhoz, it was-- I had friends.
I had also some Jewish people, like friends.
I have a friend living in Israel now that go back all the way back from that kolkhoz.
And we went to school together.
And I had Russian friends as well.
I started school.
I also developed bronchial problems.
And I had chronic bronchitis for five years.
School?
That was my first school.
And school was great, I mean, I loved school.
It wasn't till I came to Canada that I realized that school is a different sort of thing than what we had in Russia.
For one thing, in the wintertime it was very cold.
It wasn't heated, or if it was, it was the pot belly in each classroom.
We didn't have ballpoint pens.
Can you imagine ball point pens?
We had ink, and we had inkwells that froze.
So you couldn't write.
So what we used to do is, sitting in our coats and with our gloves on, and boots, the [NON-ENGLISH], the desks were sort of slanted.
And they were dark.
They were black, almost like a blackboard.

And we did our problems, let's say, whether it was in, in math or it was later on in, in algebra-- they start very early, things like that in Russia-- or to write whatever you had to write with chalk on your desk.
And it was two students per desk.
It was the longer ones, not the single ones.
And this is how we did our work at school.
Because it was very cold, very, very cold.
I liked school, yes.
I liked school.
I also had to join the [NON-ENGLISH], which is the first stage in the Communist Party.
As a school girl, everybody had to-- all the girls, all the boys.
And it's-- the [NON-ENGLISH] is the first.
Just like scouts, I suppose.
You had to wear white shirt with a red-- [NON-ENGLISH]--
Kerchief with a little emblem, it was [NON-ENGLISH], you know, ready, always ready; you know, that sort of thing.
And that's where I had, of course, made more friends.
And was-- it was OK.
It was OK, other than the odd time I was called zhyd.
And there was this [NON-ENGLISH], you know.
Not a nice word for Jew.
Jew, Jew on a string, dancing on a string or running on a string.
But I was called a lot of times, yevreyka, which is another word for Jewess.
A politer form.
But I didn't know.
What made me different?
Why did they call me yevreyka?
I went to school, I lived in the same-- I, I looked the same.
There was-- there was no difference.
My, my Russian was perfect.
I didn't know that I didn't know Russian.
You know, I mean it was-- everything was just great, and I couldn't understand why I was called yevreyka.
And there was this underlying accepted, not accepted, as part of a group.
And I was growing-- you know, I was five and six, and seven, you know.
I mean this was very formative years.
And it was something that you feel very intensely as a child.
I remember one time being called-- everybody at Christmas, I mean Christmas was a big holiday.
We didn't celebrate Christmas.
We didn't have a tree.
And that made me different.
OK?
And the children say, you don't have a tree.
Now, I had to go and ask my parents.
[NON-ENGLISH]?
What is this Jewess?
And why don't we have a tree?
And I remember one day being called in by a friend to their house to see the tree, and to partake of their, their meal.
And having to-- and they asked me to recite, because I did like poems.
And I-- I recited for them poetry, Russian poetry.
And, and, you know, they applauded, and being accepted.
I came away feeling so warm, you know, that I was accepted by them.
But yet the next day they'd call me yevreyka.
Now, it just didn't make sense.
Here you were accepted.
The next day you're called yevreyka.
They told me, it's not such a bad thing, the yevreyka.
You're, you're a Jewess.
Mother is yevreyka.
[NON-ENGLISH], I am a Jew, you know, we're all Jewish.
Now, listen, if my mom and dad are Jewish, that couldn't possibly be thought that bad for me.
But I had no Jewish knowledge.
I had no Jewish learning.
I had no idea about this, none at all, definitely nothing with religion.
It wasn't until my father was imprisoned, and we lost that house, we were made to move, that we had to find lodgings.
So we had to go to another part of this village.
And we had to wait until the cow had the calf.
And then the people took the calves into their homes, into their-- into their homes, they used to take the-- their baby livestock.
And whitewashed this place, and just remove all the manure, and put in a table and a chair, and a bed, and moved into that place.
To the best, what I recollect in the stories that I have heard-- are, of course-- elaborated, of course.
But at that time, all I know is I came home from school one day, and I started to do my homework.
And in the middle of the afternoon, my father came home with two NKVD men.
And he said to me in Yiddish, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm leaving for 10 years.
And they got very upset for him speaking Yiddish.
He had to speak Russian to me.
They searched the house.
They went into sort of the potato cellar, went through the whole thing.
And then they took him away.
And he had forgotten his cigarette paper.
And I ran after him, down towards the river.
It was a path.
That's where they took him.
And I called to him, he turned, I gave him the paper.
He hugged me.
He kissed me, and they left.
And that was in 1943.
It was after--
That was in a summer month, maybe June, maybe May.
Well when we-- when I ran to get my mother to tell her that they took away my father, with great panic and trepidation, she came home.
And all of a sudden we found out that four other men, Jewish men that were in the village, were also taken away.
And later on they said that father and they were imprisoned for political reasons.
So whatever political reasons in those days, which I understood later, didn't really have to be substantiated by anything.
And they said that my father was a spy.
And that's why they took him away.
First of all, they kept him in the village there for a very short time.
And then they sent them all the way to Petropavlovsk, which is, again, in the northern part of Russia.
And my mother and a friend of hers that little girl that-- Rachel, my friend, they each took turns looking after each other's child while one of them went to take care packages to the prisons.
And my father was interrogated for a long time, and he has some horrendous stories to tell.
They wouldn't let him sleep.
They would interrogate him through the night.
They would interrogate him through the day.
The beatings that he got, the sentencing that was-- that was a farce, pretty horrendous stories he does tell.
None of them were.
We were so isolated from everything in the village.
We had a radio, which consisted of a plate, at the corner in the room up near the ceiling, whatever was broadcast.
That's what you could hear.
You had no choices.
You had no dials.
There was a newspaper.
It was the Russian newspaper.
We had no contact with other cities, no mail, nothing.
We didn't know what was going on in the world other than the war was going on.
We--
There was an army hospital in that, that village, and it was the Russians, the Russian soldiers.
The stories that we heard were the Russian stories.
How could my father have been politically involved?
He never left the village for a minute, nobody came.
So it was just the five Jewish men, and all five of them were arrested.
None of them came back, except for my father.
How he survived, is sometimes-- I listen to the stories-- is beyond me.
Tell me how life continued for you and your mother.
Well, we lived for a little while in that little house.
My mother was an usherette by now.
She had graduated to an usherette in the movie house.
It wasn't a movie house as we know here.
It was the big gathering hall with tables, and chairs, and benches, and whatever; a screen, and one of those cameras, which in the wintertime-- do I have to tell you?
It can break-- and that celluloid kept breaking.
It took forever till it got fixed.
And because there was no babysitter, I used to go to see all those movies.
I think I mentioned to you that the first movie that I saw was [NON-ENGLISH], and I saw it again in Canada.
I was flabbergasted, I couldn't get over that Sonja Henie could speak English.
[LAUGHS] I was so sure that she was a Russian actress.
So-- and the words were translated.
The songs were also, you know, in Russian, the Russians-- [NON-ENGLISH], which was, I know why and so do you.
You know, that song.
And it was really interesting to see that such things happen.
So I was there with mom most of the nights.
But it really wasn't a very good thing to come home at 2 or 3 o'clock in the morning and have to go to school.
So we found another Jewish family.
And there was a young man and his aging mother.
He was not imprisoned.
And they moved in with us.
We took a bigger room, a bigger house, a bigger room; that's all it was.
But it was really a bad one.
Because I do remember getting dressed to go to bed, getting dressed to go to bed, mittens, gloves, a, a hat.
The one thing that I do remember having with-- oh, I should have told you this a long time-- was this [NON-ENGLISH].
Do you know that I have two eiderdown covers at the cottage made from that [NON-ENGLISH] from 1939.
It was life-saving.
In the morning, the water that I used to bring from the river for washing, for cooking, to get washed in, was frozen in the pail.
The snow and the ice would come in between the bricks.
Now--oh, that's another thing.
The houses were built out of a certain kind of brick.
The brick was made in the summertime.
It was made of manure, straw, and clay.

And people would stomp like they do grapes, on this thing, and then fill squares-- oh, let's say about maybe a foot by half a foot, some of them bigger, some of them smaller-- and then put on an angle against the sun to dry.
And as they dried, they shrank.
And they fell out of these things.
This is was what was built.
And then they were whitewashed.
Then some of them were built out of logs, and filled with that.
OK?
Now, I don't have to tell you what happened to these houses in rains.
They were incredible, that I do remember.
And I do remember the snow and the ice on the inside [LAUGHS] of the walls.
It was pretty rough.
So we lived with them.
And that is where I first started learning about religion, this aging lady.
She had a Bible.
She had a-- a Sefer Torah with her.
And she started to tell me stories.
And I was mesmerized by them.
I, I couldn't get the fill of her.
And she would tell me stories in her-- in her nice, soft, Yiddish way.
And she was like another grandmother.
She would sit near the window, and I'd sit at the stool, and she'd tell me all sorts of stories.
To me, they were stories.
It wasn't religion.
It was stories.
And that was my first inkling of that.
And that lasted till, till after we left.
What about Jewish holidays?
Did you learn about them from her?
Stories, but no observances.
We, we didn't observe.
There wasn't even 10 people to have a minion.
Now I work for a Jewish synagogue.
I mean, and for a Holocaust Jewish synagogue to begin with, I can tell you all sorts about the holidays.
In those days, I didn't know anything.
Not until we came to Germany that that became clearer, what Judaism, holidays, what it was all about.
And in 1945, when the war ended, again, these cattle cars were provided.
And the Polish citizens who did not take the Russian passports out, were allowed to leave Russia.
The whole idea was that they kept offering to the Polish people to become Russian citizens.
Some of them did.
Some of them intermarried.
And, and they had to stay.
They stayed in, in Russia.
But all those who did not take the-- out the Russian citizenship, via passports were permitted to leave Russia and go back to Poland.
Now this [NON-ENGLISH] that we were on headed for Poland, was destined for the Polish corridor, for the place that previously was Germany.
And they wanted to settle with Polish citizens that part of previous Germany.
And I can't remember how long, but it was certainly several weeks where we slept on straw and ate whatever we could, and the cattle cars were also provided with potbellied stoves.
You know, it was fairly cold.
And we got as-- into Poland, and my mother and her friend Tosia, and Rachel, and I; the four of us, my mother jumped off the train when it hit [?
Walpshich. ?] That was already the last city before that Polish corridor.
We got off, and they took us off.
And we-- the train continued.
And we stayed in [?
Walpshich. ?] And that was a revelation.
That was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like being born at the age of nine.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stores with toys.
We played in Russia with broken china pieces, you know the floral dishes, and they had little-- little flowers on them, broken pieces of China.
Those were the toys.
And collecting them, you know, and trading them, that was-- what other-- we used to put together like this the earth, and make rooms in a house.
It wasn't anything tangible.
It was just there came a wind, and you had no house.
You made it all over again.
You know, but these were the toys.
Here, walking on the street, running back and forth from side to side, not paying attention to cars, not paying attention to street cars.
We didn't know what they were.
And seeing food stores.
And seeing, you know, meat hanging, cheeses looking in the window.
Two wild Indians, two-- our mothers couldn't contain us.
We were-- [LAUGHS]
Oh.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got-- I mean, all this time in Russia, in Russia you go behind the barn, or you make from the sunflower stalks.
You know, in the summertime you tie them together for a little privacy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got an apartment in [?
Walpshich. ?] And I thought they were really uncivilized.
I mean I really thought that was-- that, that was the end.
Can you imagine being too lazy to go outside?
They had-- they had special places in-- in the house-- in the house.
Magic, with a turn of the handle, switches where I remember with, you know, turning not up-and-down switches, but with the turn of the hand, lights came on.
I do this with my grandchildren when they're about six months old, you know, maybe less, they know how to turn up and down the lights.
And they know what lights are, that this represents that.
But we-- we thought it was magic, absolute magic.
And you know what else they had?
They had paper, special paper in the bathrooms.
It was really-- until you learn what, what these things are, how to go into a store to to shop.
Because my mother worked, and she would leave me some money, and I would go for lunch, to buy my lunch.
And with money, I'd go in, and I'd buy 10 [NON-ENGLISH], you know, or a bun, or whatever.
And, and that was-- that was unusual.
Two things were very bad.
One was incredible.
In the bathroom of the apartment, we found soap.
They were like the bars of soap that you use, lye or whatever, for heavy duty soap, not facial soap.
And it was RJF soap, engraved in it.
And it wasn't until later that we found out, it stood for Rein Juden Fett.
That's when the whole picture started to unravel what did happen in our absence in Russia.
We were not aware what was going on.
But then we started to hear.
People started to congregate.
I started to hear what really did happen, and what this soap really meant.
I cannot tell you what it does to your insides.
I heard later on that a cousin of mine was killed by 17 Russians after the war, way after the war, who accosted her and killed her.
How many people were killed after the war when they returned to their homeland, Poland?
We were among the lucky ones.
Anyways, I started school there in Poland.
The other one was school.
[LAUGHS] Now, by now, I knew that I was Jewish.
OK.
And I knew that we were different.
And we went to school.
Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] School was Catholic schools.
I mean, that is the religion of the country.
And Rachel and I were taken to school.
And every morning you had to stand up, or kneel down rather, and say [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, to cross yourself.
And I wouldn't.
OK.
